courses.description: This course examines the US Constitution through the study of landmark Supreme Court cases. Students will analyze the fundamental principles of constitutional law, including federalism, separation of powers, individual rights, and civil liberties. Through case studies, simulations, and debates, students will explore how the Constitution is interpreted and applied to contemporary issues.

courses.name: Global Relations
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the study of international relations, exploring the interactions between nations, international organizations, and non-state actors. Students will examine key concepts such as sovereignty, diplomacy, conflict, and cooperation, and analyze contemporary global issues including security, human rights, and economic development.

courses.name: Law in American Society
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course introduces students to the foundations and functions of the American legal system. Students will explore criminal and civil law, the court system, and the role of law in shaping social justice. Through case studies, simulations, and discussions, students will analyze legal reasoning and the impact of law on individuals and society.

courses.name: Psychology
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course introduces students to the scientific study of human behavior and mental processes. Students will explore topics such as the brain, learning, memory, development, personality, and psychological disorders. Through discussions, case studies, and research, students will develop an understanding of psychological principles and their application to everyday life.

courses.name: Sociology
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with a chance to examine human social interaction and social organization. Sociology focuses on special topics such as groups, culture, socialization, deviance and inequalities (gender, social class, race, ethnicity). This class will explore these areas through sociological readings and engaging in discussing and simulation.

courses.name: World Religions
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course is designed to introduce students to the major religions of the world. Students will analyze each religion's foundations, historical development, principle themes and beliefs and cultural impact. Its primary purpose is to equip students with an understanding of the major ideas and practices of the world's religions so that they may better understand the world in which they live.

courses.name: AP European History
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course focuses on dominant ideas in the world today (democracy, industrialism, capitalism, communism, the scientific method) through the advanced study of European history, with a focus on understanding how European ideas, culture, politics and economics have shaped the world from 1450 to the present.

courses.name: AP Human Geography
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The purpose of the advanced placement course in human geography is to introduce students to the systematic study of patterns and processes that have shaped human understanding, use and alteration of our world. Students employ geographical and spatial concepts and tools to analyze topics such as human population movement and organization, cultural patterns and processes, political organization, agricultural and rural land use, industrialization and economic development and city and urban land use.

courses.name: AP African American Studies
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This year-long Social Studies elective credit course is designed to prepare students to meet the AP College Board interdisciplinary standards on their assessment. The course is designed to examine a diversity of African American experiences using authentic and varied sources of information. Students will study topics from early African kingdoms to the ongoing challenges and accomplishments of the present day.

courses.name: AP Psychology
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The purpose of an advanced placement course in psychology is to introduce students to the systematic and scientific study of the behavior and mental processes of human beings and other animals. Students are exposed to the psychological facts, principles and phenomena associated with each of the major subfields within psychology.

courses.name: AP Psychology–Blended
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course is a blended version of AP Psychology, combining in-person instruction with online learning components. The purpose remains to introduce students to the systematic and scientific study of behavior and mental processes.

courses.name: AP US History
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The AP US History course focuses on the development of historical thinking skills (chronological reasoning, comparing and contextualizing, crafting historical arguments using historical evidence and interpreting and synthesizing historical narrative) and an understanding of content objectives and themes. Students will explore American history from pre-Columbian times to the present.

courses.name: American Studies (AP US History and AP English Language and Composition)
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: Students are scheduled for consecutive periods of history and English, allowing for an integrated study of the two disciplines, development of skills for informed citizenship and thematic movements in our country's history and literature. This course fulfills the College Board's objectives for both AP English Language and Composition and AP US History.

courses.name: Economics
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: Students in this course develop the economic awareness and decision-making skills to make rational and informed economic decisions in life, become responsible consumers and develop essential business and job skills for their futures. Through collaborative and interactive activities and units, students gain an understanding of basic economic concepts, the impact economic factors can have on their lives and their role in the growing global economy.

courses.name: Sociology
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: Sociology is a course in partnership with Loyola University Chicago which allows students to take a college class while still in high school. As an introductory survey course, Sociology aims to provide students with an understanding of how sociologists do research and what the seminal pieces of research in their field reveal. The class will explore the structures of society, especially family, education, peers and media, all of which influence individuals by creating and maintaining culture and social stability.

courses.name: AP Macroeconomics
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The purpose of an advanced placement course in macroeconomics is to give students a thorough understanding of the principles of economics that apply to an economic system as a whole. The course places particular emphasis on the study of national income and price-level determination, and also develops students' familiarity with economic performance measures, the financial sector, stabilization policies, economic growth and international economics.

courses.name: AP Microeconomics
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This is an advanced placement level course preparing students for the AP Microeconomics exam. Coursework in AP Microeconomics includes: the basic economic problem, the nature of markets, the firm, the factor markets and the role of government.

courses.name: Philosophy and Political Theory
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: What is the meaning of life? What is reality? How do I know what I know? This course introduces philosophies and political theories by which humans have attempted to understand and organize their world. It is a college-level elective that explores major world philosophies, their influence on each other and their influence on the present.

courses.name: AP Comparative Government and Politics
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The AP Comparative Government and Politics course introduces students to the rich diversity of political life outside the United States. The course uses a comparative approach to examine the political structures; policies; and political, economic, and social challenges of six selected countries.

courses.name: AP Comparative Government and Politics Online
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This online version of AP Comparative Government and Politics introduces students to the rich diversity of political life outside the United States. The course uses a comparative approach to examine the political structures; policies; and political, economic, and social challenges of six selected countries.

courses.name: AP Government–United States
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: The AP US Government and Politics course provides a college-level, nonpartisan introduction to key political concepts, ideas, institutions, policies, interactions, roles, and behaviors that characterize the constitutional system and political culture of the United States.

courses.name: AP Government-United States Online
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This online version of AP US Government and Politics provides a college-level, nonpartisan introduction to key political concepts, ideas, institutions, policies, interactions, roles, and behaviors that characterize the constitutional system and political culture of the United States.

courses.name: US History
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: This course is a survey of American history from the colonial era to the present. Students will explore key events, figures, and movements that have shaped the nation's political, economic, social, and cultural development. Emphasis is placed on developing historical thinking, analyzing primary and secondary sources, and understanding the complexities of the American experience.

courses.name: Government
courses.subject: Social Studies
courses.description: Topics considered in this course include the fundamental concepts and structure of federal, state and local government; methods of selecting candidates for office; methods by which individuals and groups may influence government officials and mechanics of voting. Instruction and assessment will address the principles of representative government, as enunciated in the American Declaration of Independence, the US Constitution and the Constitution of the State of Illinois.
